title: Performance Tuning & Code Pushdown
description: Performance tuning at Staff+ level is not about micro-optimizations. It's about designing systems that scale — reducing data movement, leveraging HANA's compute capabilities, minimizing work process utilization, and building a performance culture. This guide covers the full spectrum from code pushdown philosophy to Staff+ performance review processes.

sections.title: Code Pushdown Philosophy

description.value:
Code pushdown is the single most important performance concept in modern ABAP. Before HANA, the database was slow (disk-based row store), and the application server was fast. The natural architecture was: fetch minimal data, process in ABAP (LOOP, aggregate, filter). HANA reversed this — the database is now 10-100x faster than the application server for data-heavy operations. Yet many ABAP programs still follow the old pattern: SELECT * from table, transfer millions of rows to the app server, then filter and aggregate in ABAP loops.

Pushdown: Move computation to where the data lives — the HANA database. CDS views push down filtering, joining, aggregation. AMDP methods push down complex procedural logic. Table functions push down calculations. The app server receives only the result — minimal data transfer, minimal processing.

Pull-up (Anti-Pattern): Fetch raw data from HANA, transfer over the network to the application server, and process in ABAP. This wastes HANA's column-store performance, increases network bandwidth, and consumes work process resources for non-value-adding computation.

Staff+ Perspective: Code pushdown is not a performance trick — it's a fundamental architectural principle. It's the same concept that drives modern data platforms (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks): compute moves to data, not the reverse. When you design an ABAP solution, always ask: "Can HANA compute this?" If yes, push it down.

sections.codeBlock:
FORM bad_pull_up.
* Anti-pattern: Fetch all rows, filter in ABAP
  SELECT * FROM zsalesorder
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_orders).        " 1M rows read

  LOOP AT lt_all_orders INTO DATA(ls_order)
    WHERE status = 'A'
      AND created_date >= lv_start_date.
    lv_sum = lv_sum + ls_order-net_amount.
  ENDLOOP.
* Transferred 1M rows over network. Filtering in ABAP.
ENDFORM.

FORM good_push_down.
* Good pattern: Pushdown filter & aggregation to HANA
  SELECT SUM( net_amount ) AS total_amount
    FROM zsalesorder
    WHERE status = 'A'
      AND created_date >= @lv_start_date
    INTO @DATA(lv_total).
* Transferred 1 row. All filtering & aggregation in HANA.
ENDFORM.

* CDS pushdown example:
@EndUserText.label: 'Pushed-down Aggregation'
define view Z_SalesByStatus
  as select from zsalesorder
{
  status,
  count(*) as order_count,
  sum(net_amount) as total_amount
}
group by status

* Consumer:
SELECT * FROM Z_SalesByStatus INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_summary).
* Aggregation happens in HANA. Only 3-10 rows returned.

decisionTree.prompt: Should this computation be pushed down to HANA or executed in the application server?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is HANA capable of executing this computation with equivalent or better performance?" If yes, push down. If the computation needs ABAP-specific runtime (ALV, field symbols, RFC calls), keep on app server.

branches.condition: Set-based aggregation, filtering, joining across large tables
branches.path: Push down to CDS view or AMDP. HANA's column store and parallel execution make set-based operations 10-100x faster than ABAP loops.

branches.condition: Procedural logic with branching, error handling, or external calls
branches.path: Keep on app server. AMDP can handle some procedural logic via SQLScript, but if you need RFC calls, message handling, or dynamic ABAP, the app server is the right place.

branches.condition: Small dataset (<10K rows) with complex ABAP processing
branches.path: Either works. The performance difference is negligible. Consider developer productivity: if the logic is easier to write and maintain in ABAP, keep it there.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Code pushdown is not binary — use CDS for declarative pushdown, AMDP for procedural pushdown, and app server for ABAP-runtime-dependent logic. Profile to validate: if data transfer is >10MB per execution, you should push down more aggressively.

sections.title: SQL Performance Patterns

description.value:
SQL performance in ABAP is dominated by three factors: (1) How much data is transferred from the database to the application server, (2) How efficiently the database executes the query, and (3) How the application server processes the result. The biggest wins come from reducing data transfer — selecting only the columns you need, filtering early in the query, and aggregating in SQL.

SELECT * is the #1 Anti-Pattern: Every column you don't need is wasted network bandwidth and memory on the app server. With HANA's column store, selecting fewer columns also improves database scan performance (fewer columns to read from the column store). Always specify the exact columns in your SELECT list.

FOR ALL ENTRIES (FAE): FAE converts an internal table into a WHERE condition with OR clauses. It has several dangers: (1) It ignores duplicate entries — the result set may not match the input table cardinality. (2) If the input table is empty, no WHERE clause is generated — all rows are returned. (3) Performance degrades with large input tables. The alternative patterns are: JOIN (when both tables are in the DB), subquery, or using CDS views with parameters.

WHERE Pushdown: The most important optimization is to filter as early as possible. Every WHERE condition that HANA can evaluate before joining reduces the join cardinality. Put the most selective conditions first — HANA's optimizer can reorder them, but clear ordering helps readability and debugging.

sections.codeBlock:
* BEFORE: SELECT * with ABAP filtering
SELECT * FROM zsalesorder          " All columns, all rows
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all).

LOOP AT lt_all INTO DATA(ls_order)
  WHERE status = 'A'
    AND created_date >= lv_start.
  lv_sum = lv_sum + ls_order-net_amount.
  lv_count = lv_count + 1.
ENDLOOP.

* AFTER: Column-specific + SQL aggregation
SELECT SUM( net_amount ) AS total,
       COUNT(*) AS cnt
  FROM zsalesorder
  WHERE status = 'A'
    AND created_date >= @lv_start
  INTO @DATA(ls_result).
* Single row returned, all computation in HANA

* ---
* FOR ALL ENTRIES anti-pattern:
SELECT vbeln FROM zsalesorder
  INTO TABLE @lt_salesorders
  WHERE created_date >= @lv_start.

IF lt_salesorders[] IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT * FROM zsalesorder_item
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_salesorders
    WHERE vbeln = @lt_salesorders-vbeln
    INTO TABLE @lt_items.
ENDIF.

* Better: Use JOIN instead of FAE
SELECT so.vbeln, item.*
  FROM zsalesorder AS so
  INNER JOIN zsalesorder_item AS item
    ON so.vbeln = item.vbeln
  WHERE so.created_date >= @lv_start
  INTO TABLE @lt_items.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: FOR ALL ENTRIES vs JOIN vs Internal Table — Cost Comparison

fae.monthlyPenalty: $1,200 — 30 batch runs × 850ms = 25.5s. Risk: if input table empty, FAE returns ALL items (1M rows) — catastrophic. Also: duplicate handling requires DISTINCT.

join.monthlyPenalty: $360 — 30 runs × 120ms = 3.6s. Best execution plan. No empty-table risk. Database handles all optimizations.

internal_table.monthlyPenalty: $2,400 — 30 runs × 800ms = 24s. Transfers all item rows even if only 0.5% match. Network overhead dominates.

calculation.breakeven: JOIN is the default winner. FAE only justifies itself when the driving table is not in the same database (different systems via RFC). For same-DB scenarios, JOIN is always faster and safer.

sections.title: Index Strategies for HANA

description.value:
HANA's index strategy differs fundamentally from traditional databases. HANA's default storage is column-store — data is stored column-wise, enabling vectorized processing, compression, and parallel scan. Secondary indexes in HANA are sparse indexes (inverted indices) that point to column store segments. Understanding this architecture is critical for effective index design.

Covering Indexes: An index that contains all columns referenced in the query (SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY). The database can satisfy the query entirely from the index without accessing the table. In HANA, covering indexes are especially powerful because the column store can scan only the relevant index columns.

Composite Index Column Order: The leftmost prefix rule applies — an index on (A, B, C) can optimize queries filtering on A, (A, B), or (A, B, C), but not on B or C alone. Order columns by selectivity (most selective first) to minimize the index segment scanned.

Inverted Indexes for Text: HANA's fulltext search uses inverted indexes — a mapping from words to document IDs. These enable fuzzy search, stemming, and ranked results. Create fulltext indexes on columns used with CONTAINS() or LIKE '%pattern%' queries.

sections.codeBlock:
* Create a covering index for the sales order query
* All query columns (SalesOrg, Status, NetAmount) are in the index
CREATE INDEX z_idx_salesorder_cov
  ON zsalesorder ( sales_org, status, net_amount )
  ASCENDING;

* Query that benefits from this index:
SELECT SUM( net_amount ) AS total
  FROM zsalesorder
  WHERE sales_org = 'US00'
    AND status = 'A';
* HANA reads only the index columns — no table access needed.

* ---
* Composite index — column order matters
* GOOD (selectivity first):
CREATE INDEX z_idx_order_good
  ON zsalesorder ( status, created_date, sales_org );
* Query: WHERE status = 'A' AND created_date >= ...
* Index narrows by status first (10% rows), then date range.

* BAD (low selectivity first):
CREATE INDEX z_idx_order_bad
  ON zsalesorder ( created_date, status, sales_org );
* Query: WHERE status = 'A' AND created_date >= ...
* Index scans wide date range first, then filters by status.

* ---
* Fulltext index for text search
CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX z_fti_customer
  ON zcustomer ( customer_name, search_term )
  CONFIGURATION 'EXTRACTOR_CORE';

* Query:
SELECT * FROM zcustomer
  WHERE CONTAINS( customer_name, 'smith' )
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_found).

rows: Queries that reference only index columns
High-frequency queries where table access must be avoided
Query WHERE patterns are well-known and selective
comparisonTable.takeaway: HANA's column store reduces the need for many secondary indexes. Profile with ST05 PlanViz before adding indexes — often HANA's implicit indexes suffice. Composite indexes should match the query WHERE pattern exactly. Fulltext indexes are expensive — use only on columns that genuinely need text search.

sections.title: ABAP Runtime Profiling

description.value:
Runtime profiling is the foundation of performance optimization. Without data, optimization is guesswork. ABAP provides multiple profiling tools: SAT (ABAP Trace) for end-to-end analysis, SE30 for single-report profiling, ST05 for SQL and RFC trace, and PlanViz for HANA execution plan visualization. At Staff+ level, you must read these traces systematically and identify optimization opportunities.

Reading SAT Traces: SAT (transaction SE30 / SAT) records every ABAP statement, database operation, and function call. Key metrics: (1) Database time vs ABAP time — the ratio tells you whether the bottleneck is SQL or processing. (2) Number of database calls — each call has overhead even if small. Batch operations DRAMATICALLY reduce roundtrips. (3) Internal table operations — LOOP, INSERT, APPEND on large tables can dominate ABAP time.

SQL Buffer Analysis: Every database call goes through HANA's result set cache (SQL buffer). Repeated identical queries benefit from cache hits. SAT shows cache hit ratios. Low hit ratios suggest generating dynamic WHERE clauses (FAE, SELECT with variable conditions) that prevent caching.

RFC Roundtrip Optimization: Each RFC call adds network latency + serialization/deserialization overhead. SAT records RFC call counts and durations. Batch RFC calls into table input/output parameters instead of per-line calls. For large data transfers, use streams or IDoc segmentation.

sections.codeBlock:
* SAT/SE30 trace analysis — manual evaluation steps

* 1. Run SAT on your report / transaction
* 2. Go to: Hit List (transaction-wide) → Sort by Total Time DESC
* 3. Check: Database time ratio vs ABAP time ratio
*    - DB > 70%: Focus on SQL optimization (indexes, pushdown, FAE)
*    - ABAP > 70%: Focus on ABAP processing (LOOP, internal tables)
*
* 4. Identify top 5 database operations by total time
*    - Check: Full table scan? (no WHERE clause)
*    - Check: SELECT * fetching unused columns?
*    - Check: FAE generating thousands of individual SELECTs?
*
* 5. Identify top 5 ABAP operations by total time
*    - Check: LOOP inside LOOP (nested loops on large tables)
*    - Check: Frequent APPEND or INSERT to large internal tables
*    - Check: Excessive string operations (CONCATENATE, SPLIT)

* ---
* Before optimization SAT output (example):
" Database time: 8,234ms (82%)
" ABAP time:     1,800ms (18%)
" Top hit: SELECT from ZSALESORDER_ITEM (5,400ms, 800 calls via FAE)

* After optimization (JOIN + covering index):
" Database time: 711ms (45%)
" ABAP time:     870ms (55%)
" Top hit: SELECT from ZSALESORDER_ITEM (320ms, 1 call via JOIN)

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Profiling Investment — Time Spent vs Performance Gained

profile_fix.writeLatency: 8 hours profiling + 4 hours fix = 12 hours total
profile_fix.monthlyPenalty: $1,440 investment. If runtime drops from 45 min to 5 min: saves 40 min × 12 runs = 8 hours/year. ROI = (8 × $120) / (12 × $120) = 0.67x. Marginal.

skip_profile.monthlyPenalty: $240 investment. 80% chance the guess-fix doesn't help. Likely outcome: no improvement, or regression. Worst case: incorrect index slows other queries.

calculation.breakeven: Profiling pays off when: (1) Report runs daily (not monthly) — ROI jumps to 8x+. (2) Multiple reports share the same bottleneck — one profile fixes 5 reports. (3) Database server is CPU-bound — reducing one report's load benefits all concurrent users. Rule: if a report runs weekly or more often, profile. If monthly, profile only if it's the bottleneck in the batch window.

sections.title: CDS View Performance

description.value:
CDS view performance depends on how the HANA optimizer translates the view definition into an execution plan. Unlike traditional views (which are simply inlined), CDS views undergo extensive optimization — predicate pushdown, association resolution, column pruning. But certain patterns prevent optimization and force the worst-case execution.

Association vs JOIN: Associations enable lazy resolution — the framework decides whether to resolve the association via JOIN or separate SELECT. This sounds good, but incorrect cardinality annotations can cause the optimizer to choose the wrong strategy. A [1..1] association that's actually [0..1] may cause an INNER JOIN that silently filters out valid rows. Always verify cardinality annotations match the actual data.

CDS Aggregation Pushdown: When a CDS view uses SUM, COUNT, MAX, or MIN, the aggregation is pushed to HANA's column store. This is extremely efficient — HANA can aggregate in-memory without materializing intermediate rows. The consumer receives only the aggregated result, not the raw data.

UNION vs UNION ALL: UNION removes duplicates (requiring a sort), while UNION ALL preserves duplicates (pure concatenation). If you don't need deduplication, use UNION ALL — it avoids the sort overhead. For large result sets, UNION ALL can be 3-5x faster than UNION.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Performance-Optimized CDS View'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@Environment.systemField: #SYSTEM_DATE

define view Z_PerformanceOptimized
  with parameters
    P_SalesOrg : vkorg,
    P_DateFrom : dats
  as select from zsalesorder as so
    inner join zcustomer as c
      on so.customer_id = c.customer_id
{
  key so.vbeln                as SalesOrderID,
      c.name1                 as CustomerName,
      so.net_amount           as NetAmount,
      so.created_date         as CreatedDate,
      /*
        Aggregation in CDS — pushes to HANA
      */
      cast( so.net_amount as fltp ) * 1.1 as AdjustedAmount
}
where
  so.sales_org = $parameters.P_SalesOrg and
  so.created_date >= $parameters.P_DateFrom

* UNION vs UNION ALL:
* Use UNION ALL unless you explicitly need deduplication

define view Z_OrdersCurrent
  as select from zsalesorder where status = 'A';

define view Z_OrdersArchive
  as select from zarch_orders where status = 'A';

define view Z_CombinedOrders
  as select from Z_OrdersCurrent
  union all
  select from Z_OrdersArchive;
* UNION ALL: 120ms, 500K rows
* UNION:     540ms, 500K rows (sort for dedup adds 420ms)

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use an association or a JOIN in this CDS view?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Will the consumer always need the related data at query time?" If always needed → JOIN (eager). If conditionally needed → Association (lazy). If data is never needed in SQL — use path expression or $expand.

branches.condition: Consumer always reads the related entity (bulk report, analytics)
branches.path: JOIN. Eager resolution is cheaper when data is always needed. The optimizer can push predicates through JOINs. Use INNER JOIN if both sides always exist, LEFT OUTER if optional.

branches.condition: Consumer only sometimes accesses related data (Fiori UI, OData $expand)
branches.path: Association with correct cardinality annotation. The lazy resolution avoids unnecessary data transfer. For OData, $expand triggers association resolution only when requested.

branches.condition: Related data is needed only in the SELECT list (path expression)
branches.path: Association with path expression in SELECT. The optimizer decides at plan time whether to flatten into a JOIN or execute a separate SELECT. Verify the generated plan in PlanViz.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Associations are NOT always better than JOINs. The lazy resolution of associations adds overhead for each resolution. If you always access the related data, a JOIN is cheaper. Profile the generated SQL in PlanViz to verify the optimizer's choice.

sections.title: Memory Management & Caching

description.value:
ABAP memory management spans multiple layers: roll area (per dialog step), paging (extended memory across steps), heap memory (dynamic allocation), and shared memory (cross-transaction buffers). Understanding this hierarchy is critical for preventing memory bottlenecks, particularly in batch jobs and high-throughput dialog transactions.

Internal Table Memory: Internal tables are allocated in the work process's heap memory. Large internal tables (>100K rows) can exhaust the work process heap, causing a SWAP_LOCATION error. Use PACKED or STANDARD tables with appropriate initial size. For very large datasets, process in chunks (UP TO ... ROWS) instead of loading everything into memory.

Table Buffering: Table buffering caches database table contents in shared memory (buffer area). The buffering types are: (1) Single Record — only the requested record is cached. Smallest memory footprint but per-record overhead. (2) Generic — key prefix is cached (e.g., all records for a given sales org). Good balance. (3) Full — entire table is cached. Only for small tables (<1,000 rows). (4) Area — similar to full but with expiration. Buffering is configured in SE13/SE14 on the table definition.

Buffer Synchronization: When a buffered table is modified, the buffer on the current application server is invalidated. Other application servers' buffers become stale until their next read. For frequently updated tables, avoid buffering — the invalidation overhead exceeds the read benefit. Configure buffer change settings on the table: buffersync = transaction or buffersync = functionmodule.

sections.codeBlock:
* Table buffering configuration (SE13 / technical settings):
* For configuration table ZTCONFIG (< 500 rows):
*   Buffering: Full buffering
*   Buffer change: Sync on database update
*
* For master data table ZTCUSTOMER (50K rows, frequently read):
*   Buffering: Generic buffering (by SalesOrg key)
*   Buffer area: 2MB per buffer
*
* For transaction table ZTSALESORDER (5M rows, frequently written):
*   Buffering: No buffering (write access invalidates buffer)

* Memory-safe pattern — process in chunks:
DATA: lv_offset TYPE i VALUE 0,
      lv_chunk  TYPE i VALUE 10000.

WHILE lv_offset >= 0.
  SELECT * FROM zpayment_log
    WHERE created_date >= lv_start
    ORDER BY payment_id
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_chunk)
    UP TO @lv_chunk ROWS
    OFFSET @lv_offset.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    lv_offset = -1.  " No more rows
    CONTINUE.
  ENDIF.

  LOOP AT lt_chunk INTO DATA(ls_payment).
*   Process each payment in memory-safe chunk
    lv_total = lv_total + ls_payment-amount.
  ENDLOOP.

  lv_offset = lv_offset + lv_chunk.
ENDWHILE.
* Memory: Only 10K rows in heap at any time, not the full 2M rows

adversarialResponse.challenge: "We keep getting SWAP_LOCATION errors in production. Just increase the work process memory limits. That's the easiest fix."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You shouldn't increase work process memory. It might destabilize the system." (says no but offers no alternative)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I understand the appeal of fixing SWAP_LOCATION by increasing zdumping_size — it's a 5-minute config change. But let me explain why that's a safety net, not a solution. Work process memory is shared across all work processes on that application server. Increasing the limit means less memory available for other work processes — you risk causing SWAP_LOCATION errors in ALL processes instead of one. Additionally, the root cause is almost always an unconstrained internal table read — a SELECT with no WHERE clause or a FAE with an empty input table. The fix is systematic: (1) Identify the specific program causing the error using ST22 — the error log shows the exact statement (likely a SELECT or LOOP on a table with >1M rows). (2) Add an explicit check: if the input table exceeds 100K rows, log a warning and process in chunks. (3) Set a memory limit in code using the LIMIT option on SELECT. Let me find the root cause first — 90% of SWAP_LOCATION errors are fixed by adding a WHERE clause, not increasing memory."
adversarialResponse.pattern: root-cause-vs-symptom — acknowledge the quick-fix appeal, explain the side effects (memory cannibalization), and redirect to systematic root cause analysis (ST22 dump, add WHERE, chunk processing).

sections.title: HANA-Specific Tuning

description.value:
HANA-specific tuning goes beyond standard SQL optimization. HANA's column store, in-memory architecture, and parallel execution engine offer unique optimization opportunities. Understanding these features is essential for achieving 10x query performance improvements over standard SQL patterns.

PAQ Tables (Planning and Analytics Queries): PAQ tables are lookup tables stored in HANA's column store for rapid aggregation. They are particularly optimized for OLAP queries with large fact tables and multiple dimensions. Instead of joining fact tables at query time, PAQ tables pre-join the data for specific analytical scenarios. Use PAQ tables for dashboards and analytical reports with predictable dimension combinations.

Plan Stability: The HANA optimizer chooses an execution plan based on table statistics, available indexes, and estimated cardinality. If statistics are stale or data distribution changes, the optimizer may choose a suboptimal plan. Plan stability allows you to "freeze" a known-good execution plan for a specific query. This is the HANA equivalent of SQL Server's query plan guides or Oracle's stored outlines.

Stored Procedures vs Functions: HANA stored procedures and functions execute directly on the HANA server, eliminating data transfer. Use stored procedures for complex multi-step business logic (stock calculation, MRP run). Use table functions (exposed via CDS) for repeatable calculations that return result sets. Avoid stored procedures for simple SELECT statements — the overhead of procedure call exceeds any benefit.

sections.codeBlock:
* Plan Stability — Freeze a known-good execution plan
* Prerequisite: Identify the problematic query from ST05/PlanViz

* Execute SQL to capture the plan:
SELECT * FROM zsalesorder
  WHERE sales_org = 'US00'
    AND created_date >= '20240101'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_data).

* In HANA Studio / DBA Cockpit:
* 1. Find the SQL statement in Performance → SQL Cache
* 2. Right-click → Create Plan Stability Entry
* 3. Choose: 'Capture Execution Plan'
* 4. HANA will reuse this plan even if statistics change

* ---
* HANA Trace analysis (HDBTRACE):
* Run from HANA Studio:
* ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini', 'system')
*   SET ('trace', 'sql_service') = 'DEBUG' WITH RECONFIGURE;
*
* Execute your ABAP program
* Read trace from: /usr/sap/<SID>/HDB<instance>/trace/
* Look for: 'plan_id', 'operator', 'execution_engine'
* Key patterns:
*   - 'table_scan': Full scan — missing index or WHERE
*   - 'join_scan': Join on non-key columns
*   - 'column_search': Column store lookup — good

* ---
* Stored procedure for complex calculation:
CREATE PROCEDURE Z_CALCULATE_STOCK (
  IN iv_material NVARCHAR(40),
  IN iv_plant    NVARCHAR(10),
  OUT ov_quantity DECIMAL(15,2)
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
AS
BEGIN
  DECLARE lv_stock DECIMAL(15,2);
  DECLARE lv_reserved DECIMAL(15,2);

  SELECT SUM(quantity) INTO lv_stock
    FROM zmaterial_stock
    WHERE material = :iv_material
      AND plant = :iv_plant;

  SELECT SUM(reserved_qty) INTO lv_reserved
    FROM zreservation
    WHERE material = :iv_material
      AND plant = :iv_plant;

  ov_quantity := :lv_stock - :lv_reserved;
END;

organizationalDimension.prompt: How should HANA-specific tuning knowledge be organized and transferred across the team?
challenges: HANA-specific features (plan stability, PAQ tables, stored procedures) are niche knowledge held by 1-2 senior developers
Plan stability entries are invisible to ABAP developers — they're created in HANA Studio, not in ABAP code
Different teams own different schema objects — a query plan change in one team's area can affect another team's query
organizationalDimension.teamStructure: Create a Database Tuning Center of Excellence (CoE) with representatives from each application team. Maintain a shared repository of plan stability entries and their business justification. Require an ADR for each plan stability entry documenting: (1) the query pattern, (2) why the optimizer chose a bad plan, (3) the expected plan, and (4) the monitoring metric (re-plan when cardinality changes by 20%+). Schedule monthly plan review — analyze HANA's SQL cache for new bad plans and update stability entries. Train each team's lead on reading PlanViz execution plans — they should be able to self-diagnose before escalating to CoE.
organizationalDimension.staffPlusTip: HANA-specific tuning is organizational knowledge, not individual heroics. The goal is to reduce the bus factor — every team should have someone who can read a PlanViz trace. Rotate the CoE membership every 6 months to cross-train.

sections.title: Staff+ Performance Review Process

description.value:
At Staff+ level, performance is not a technical problem — it's a cultural and process problem. Building a high-performance SAP system requires shift-left testing (find performance issues early), cost modeling (quantify the cost of inefficiency), and automated regression testing (prevent performance backslides).

Building a Performance Culture: Performance is everyone's responsibility, not just the DBA's. Embed performance review in the development workflow: (1) Every code review has a performance checklist item — is there a SELECT *? Is there an FAE that could be a JOIN? Is the data transfer minimized? (2) Every new CDS view must include a performance annotation block (@ObjectModel.usageType, cardinality annotations). (3) Every user story has an acceptance criterion for response time.

Shift-Left Performance Testing: Find performance issues during development, not in production. Integrate lightweight profiling into the CI/CD pipeline: (1) Run SAT on every unit test — if database time exceeds a threshold (e.g., 100ms), fail the build. (2) Compare the generated SQL from CDS views — a change that introduces an additional JOIN should trigger a performance review. (3) Use the ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) with custom performance checks.

Cost Modeling: Every performance problem has a cost. Model the cost of a slow query: (1) Database server cost per core-hour. (2) Work process utilization cost. (3) User productivity loss (users waiting). Present performance optimizations as cost-saving investments, not technical improvements.

sections.codeBlock:
* Performance regression test framework (pseudo-abap)

CLASS zcl_performance_test DEFINITION FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS run_salesorder_test RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE string.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA: lv_start    TYPE timestamp,
          lv_end      TYPE timestamp,
          lv_duration TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_performance_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD run_salesorder_test.
*   Measure baseline
    GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_start.

    SELECT * FROM zsalesorder INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all)
      WHERE created_date >= '20240101'.

    GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_end.
    lv_duration = ( lv_end - lv_start ) * 1000.

    IF lv_duration > 5000.  " Threshold: 5 seconds
      result = |FAIL: Query took { lv_duration }ms (threshold 5000ms)|.
    ELSE.
      result = |PASS: { lv_duration }ms|.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Integrate into CI/CD (gCTS / abapGit CI):
* 1. On every commit, run zcl_performance_test
* 2. Compare result against baseline (stored in ZPERF_BASELINE)
* 3. If degradation > 20%, fail the build
* 4. Developer must optimize before merging

* Automated performance check via ATC:
* Create custom ATC check variant:
* - Flag SELECT * without explicit column list
* - Flag FAE with > 1000 input entries
* - Flag missing WHERE clause on tables > 10K rows
* - Flag implicit commits within transaction loops

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Performance regression tests are a waste of time. We have a dedicated Basis team that monitors production performance. They'll catch any issues."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You can't rely on Basis to catch everything. Production monitoring is reactive." (negative, no alternative)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I respect the Basis team — they do critical work. But here's the limitation: production monitoring detects problems after users are affected. A report that ran in 2 seconds now takes 2 minutes — users notice immediately, but the monitoring alert triggers after the business impact. The cost is not just the slower report — it's the 20 minutes of user wait time × 500 users = 167 hours lost per incident. Regression tests in CI/CD catch these problems before release, when the fix is a 1-hour code change instead of a 2-day production emergency. Let's start small: add one performance regression test for your most critical report (the monthly financial close). If it passes for 3 months, we'll extend the approach. Basis can focus on infrastructure performance (CPU, memory, disk I/O) while our tests cover application-level performance. The two approaches are complementary, not competing."
adversarialResponse.pattern: complementary-not-competing — validate the existing approach, quantify the blind spot (production detection is reactive), propose a targeted low-risk start (one critical report), and show how the new approach complements (not replaces) existing monitoring.

details: Staff+ Insight: Performance tuning at Staff+ level is about system thinking — understanding how data moves through the entire stack (HANA → network → app server → memory → user), and designing interventions that reduce data movement at every layer. Interviewers testing performance knowledge want to see that you can read traces, quantify the business impact of inefficiency, and build organizational processes that prevent regression. The best performance optimization is the one that's never needed because the system was designed well from the start.
